1: The sending of the seventy-two (or seventy -- textual variant) represents the extension of the messianic mission beyond the Twelve. The seventy recalls the seventy elders of Israel (Numbers 11:16-17) on whom God placed the Spirit, and the table of nations in Gen 10 (seventy nations in Hebrew tradition, seventy-two in LXX). Either reading points to universality: Jesus is sending workers into a harvest that reaches all peoples. This is the missional foundation for Acts' Gentile mission -- the twelve apostles extend to Israel; the seventy point toward the nations. The harvest metaphor frames the mission in terms Jesus will use for the final ingathering (Matthew 13:39; Revelation 14:15).
2: "The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into his harvest field" -- the harvest metaphor for the gathering of souls (Isaiah 27:12; Jeremiah 51:33; Joel 3:13; Revelation 14:14-16) reaches its fulfillment in Jesus's mission. He is the Lord of the harvest who sends the workers -- and the workers are told to ask the Lord of the harvest to send more. The prayer for more workers is addressed to Jesus himself, establishing that Jesus has the authority over the harvest that the OT attributes to YHWH. The prayer creates the feedback loop that generates Acts: the disciples' prayer produces the Pentecost harvest.
3: "Go! I am sending you out like lambs among wolves" -- the imagery of the sent ones as lambs among wolves draws on Isaiah 11:6 (the peaceable Kingdom where "the wolf will live with the lamb") and Isaiah 65:25. In the present age, the sent ones are lambs in a world that has not yet received the Kingdom. The combination of genuine vulnerability ("lambs") and divine commission ("I am sending") frames the missionary vocation: it is not safe but it is authorized. Jesus sends disciples into the condition he himself will face -- the lamb led to slaughter of Isaiah 53:7.
4: "Do not take a purse or bag or sandals; and do not greet anyone on the road" -- the instruction mirrors Elisha's instruction to Gehazi (2 Kings 4:29: "Don't greet anyone you meet") when he was sent urgently to lay Elisha's staff on the dead child. The urgency communicates the eschatological moment: the Kingdom is pressing; there is no time for extended social pleasantries. The lack of provisions communicates radical dependence on the Father's hospitality provision -- the same dependence Jesus himself models. The absence of provisions is not poverty but eschatological urgency-embodied.
5: "When you enter a house, first say, 'Peace to this house.'" -- the shalom greeting (peace = wholeness, flourishing, the covenant state of restored relationship with God) is the Kingdom's announcement. In Jewish custom, "shalom" was both a greeting and a blessing. In Jesus's instruction, the peace pronouncement is not merely a social formality but a Kingdom declaration: the era of shalom that the prophets anticipated (Isaiah 52:7: "How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news, who proclaim peace") has arrived with the disciples' mission.
6: "If someone who promotes peace is there, your peace will rest on them; if not, it will return to you" -- "a person of peace" (huios eirēnēs = son of peace) is the one in whom the Kingdom announcement takes root. This prepares the reader for Acts's accounts of households receiving the Gospel (Acts 10:2, Cornelius; Acts 16:14-15, Lydia; Acts 16:31-34, the Philippian jailer). The reversible peace -- if rejected it returns to the sender -- establishes that the Gospel announcement creates a division by its reception or rejection, not merely by its proclamation.
7: "The worker deserves his wages" -- cited by Paul in 1 Corinthians 9:14 and 1 Timothy 5:18 as the basis for the church's support of its ministers. Jesus establishes this principle at the founding of the mission. The instruction not to move from house to house prevents the kind of social climbing that would compromise the mission's integrity. The messenger of the Kingdom is sustained by the Kingdom's hospitality, not by accumulating social advantage.
8: Eating whatever is set before you in a town that receives you is the early implicit move toward Gentile table fellowship that Acts 10:9-16 (the vision of unclean animals) and Luke 15:19-21 (the Jerusalem council) will make explicit. The mission's table practice anticipates the new covenant community where Jew and Gentile eat together -- the issue Paul addresses in Galatians 2:11-14 (the Antioch incident). The instruction to eat what is set before is the proto-practice of the one-new-humanity table that Christ's cross creates (Ephesians 2:14-16).
9: "Heal the sick who are there and tell them, 'The kingdom of God has come near to you.'" -- the combination of healing and proclamation is the enacted Gospel: the Kingdom comes as both word and deed. The healings are the signs of the Kingdom's arrival -- the OT expectation (Isaiah 35:5-6: "the blind see, the lame walk, the deaf hear") being fulfilled in each town. Jesus is not the only one who enacts these signs; the seventy-two carry the same Kingdom-power because they bear Jesus's name and authority. Acts 3:1-10 demonstrates the continuation of this pattern after the resurrection.
10: The towns that reject the mission are to have the Kingdom announced to them nonetheless -- "the kingdom of God has come near" is spoken even over the rejecting town. This anticipates Paul's practice of declaring the Gospel even to those who reject it (Acts 13:51; Luke 18:6). The announcement over the refusing town is the last act of grace before judgment is confirmed.
11: "Even the dust of your town we wipe from our feet as a warning to you. Yet be sure of this: the kingdom of God has come near" -- the dust-wiping gesture (practiced by Jews returning from Gentile territory) is here applied to Jewish towns that refuse Jesus's messengers. The reversal is stark: towns that receive the seventy are treated as holy space; towns that reject them are treated as spiritually unclean territory. The kingdom's arrival makes reception or rejection the decisive criterion of covenant standing, not ethnic identity.
12: "I tell you, it will be more bearable on that day for Sodom than for that town" -- Sodom's destruction (Genesis 19:24-25) was the paradigmatic divine judgment in the OT. The "that day" is the day of final judgment. Jesus's comparison establishes a principle of proportional accountability: greater revelation received and refused = greater judgment incurred. The Incarnation increases human accountability, not merely human opportunity.
13: "Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida!" -- the Gentile cities of Tyre and Sidon (condemned by OT prophets: Isa 23; Ezek 26-28; Joel 3:4-8; Amos 1:9-10) had received no messianic visitation, yet Jesus declares they would have responded better than Chorazin and Bethsaida. This is the logic of the Elijah-Zarephath and Elisha-Naaman examples of Luke 4:25-27: divine mercy is not constrained by covenant boundaries, and covenant privilege does not guarantee covenant response.
14: "But it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment than for you" -- the judgment comparisons establish a graduated accountability that the NT develops theologically. Hebrews 10:29 uses the same logic: "How much more severely do you think someone deserves to be punished who has trampled the Son of God underfoot?" The presence of the Son of God creates the highest possible stakes for human response. Every woe here is the Christological argument for human responsibility before the Gospel.
15: "And you, Capernaum, will you be lifted up to the heavens? No, you will go down to Hades" -- the language echoes Isaiah 14:13-15 (the taunt against Babylon who said "I will ascend to the heavens... I will make myself like the Most High" and was brought down to the realm of the dead). Capernaum's sin is ignoring the one greater than Babylon's king who has been present in its streets. The pride of privilege -- receiving the Messiah's headquarters ministry without genuine response -- is the spiritual equivalent of Babylonian hubris.
16: "Whoever listens to you listens to me; whoever rejects you rejects me; but whoever rejects me rejects him who sent me" -- the chain of identification (disciple to Jesus to Father) establishes the theological principle of apostolic authority. To hear the apostolic proclamation is to hear Jesus; to reject Jesus is to reject the Father. This is the foundation for the NT's claim that the Gospel word has divine authority (1 Thessalonians 2:13: "you accepted it not as a human word, but as it actually is, the word of God"). The delegation of divine authority through human messengers is the structural reality of the Incarnation continued in the church's mission.
17: "Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name!" -- the authority to cast out demons is delegated authority exercised in Jesus's name. The name of Jesus is not a magical formula but the invocation of Jesus's identity and authority as the one who has bound the strong man (Luke 11:21-22). The disciples' amazement reveals they did not know in advance the full extent of the authority they had been given. Acts 16:18 (Paul commanding a spirit to leave "in the name of Jesus Christ") demonstrates the continuation of this delegated authority after the resurrection.
18: "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" -- the statement is eschatologically momentous. Whether Jesus refers to a primordial fall (Isaiah 14:12), the present defeat of Satan through the seventy-two's mission, or the coming cross-defeat (John 12:31: "Now the prince of this world will be driven out"), the implication is the same: the Incarnation and the mission that flows from it constitutes the decisive reversal of Satan's cosmic authority. Revelation 12:7-12 presents this fall as the cosmic consequence of the Christ-event.
19: "I have given you authority to trample on snakes and scorpions and to overcome all the power of the enemy" -- the authority over serpents and scorpions draws on Psalms 91:13 and on the Genesis 3:15 promise: "I will put enmity between you and the woman... you will strike his heel." The mission of the seventy-two is part of the unfolding fulfillment of Genesis 3:15 -- the seed of the woman crushing the serpent's head. The psalm the devil quoted in the temptation (Luke 4:10-11) is now fulfilled in the disciples' mission.
20: "Do not rejoice that the spirits submit to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven" -- the eschatological book of the living (Exodus 32:32-33; Daniel 12:1; Malachi 3:16; Revelation 3:5; Luke 20:12) is the final register of the saved. Jesus redirects the seventy-two's joy from the temporary (victory over demons in the present age) to the permanent (salvation, the eternal state). The Christ-centered priority: not power demonstrations but the relationship with the Father that secures eternal life.
21: "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children" -- Luke's "Johannine thunderbolt" (Matthew 11:25-27 || Luke 10:21-22) is the most explicitly trinitarian passage in the Synoptics. Jesus addresses the Father with the Hebrew intimacy term "Abba" while using the full cosmic title "Lord of heaven and earth." The revelation to "little children" (nēpiois = infants/those without status) vs. the concealment from "the wise" is the epistemological pattern of the Incarnation: the Gospel is received by those who have no self-sufficient wisdom to resist it.
22: "All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him" -- this verse claims mutual exclusive knowledge between Father and Son that in the OT is reserved for YHWH's own self-knowledge (Isaiah 46:9-10; Luke 55:8-9). Jesus claims to be the exclusive mediator of the Father's revelation. John 14:6 ("no one comes to the Father except through me") is the direct application. This is the Christological pinnacle of the Synoptic tradition.
23: "Blessed are the eyes that see what you see" -- beatitude pronounced on the disciples as witnesses of the Kingdom's arrival. The Christological point: the disciples are in the position that all of OT history was pointing toward. Abraham, Moses, David, Isaiah, and the prophets were living in the promise; the disciples are living in the fulfillment. 1 Peter 1:10-12 makes this explicit: "the prophets who spoke of the grace that was to come to you... tried to find out the time and circumstances to which the Spirit of Christ in them was pointing."
24: "Many prophets and kings desired to see what you see but did not see it" -- the prophets' desire to see the Christ was the desire of the entire OT canonical horizon. Hebrews 11:13: "All these people were still living by faith when they died. They did not receive the things promised; they only saw them and welcomed them from a distance." David himself saw it prophetically (Acts 2:30-31: "he was a prophet and knew that God had promised him on oath that he would place one of his descendants on his throne"). The disciples stand where history had been aiming.
25: "What must I do to inherit eternal life?" -- the question frames the Good Samaritan parable within the question of salvation. The lawyer's use of "inherit" draws on the OT inheritance-language: Israel inherits the land (Numbers 26:55-56; Deuteronomy 4:21); the righteous inherit the kingdom (Psalms 37:11; Matthew 5:5). Jesus turns the question back to the Law, then to the lawyer's application, then tells a parable that redefines who is the neighbor -- and implicitly, who is the one who genuinely fulfills the law's demand.
26: "What is written in the Law? How do you read it?" -- Jesus characteristically responds to a question with a question. The lawyer's citation of the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:5) plus Leviticus 19:18 as the summary of the Law anticipates Jesus's own summary in Matthew 22:37-40. The Christological point: Jesus himself is the perfect embodiment of both commands -- complete love for the Father and complete love for neighbor, supremely demonstrated at the cross.
27: The fourfold "all" (heart / soul / strength / mind) is the total-person demand of the Shema. No division of the person is exempt from the love-obligation. Jesus is not modifying the Law but affirming its deepest demand -- a demand that no human perfectly fulfills, which is precisely why the one who perfectly fulfills it (Christ) must do so on humanity's behalf.
28: "Do this and you will live" -- the conditional response is true as stated: love God and neighbor completely = life. But it conceals the problem: who can do this completely? The subsequent parable exposes the gap through the lawyer's question "who is my neighbor?" Paul makes the same point in Galatians 3:12: "The law is not based on faith; on the contrary, it says, 'The person who does these things will live by them.'" The Law's "do this and live" is real -- but the impossibility of doing it drives the Law's function as a schoolmaster pointing to Christ (Galatians 3:24).
29: "And who is my neighbor?" -- the question was a live legal debate. The lawyer's motive ("wanting to justify himself") reveals the heart: not a genuine question but a limiting move, a desire to draw the boundary of obligation narrowly enough to be able to claim fulfillment. The Good Samaritan parable answers the question by inverting it: Jesus does not define who counts as a neighbor but asks which person acted as a neighbor. The question is not "who deserves my love?" but "whose love do you need?" Christ himself is the one who loved those who did not deserve it.
30: A man going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, robbed, beaten, and left half-dead. The parable's Christological typological reading (from Origen through Augustine): the wounded man is Adam/humanity; the priest and Levite are the Law and the sacrificial system that cannot save; the Samaritan is Christ who crosses every barrier to help. Whether or not the allegory was intended, the Christological resonance is real: only the despised outsider, the one the religious leaders avoid, provides the rescue. The esplagchnisthē (was moved with compassion: Luke 10:33) is the term used for Jesus's own compassion throughout the Gospels.
31: The priest who "passed by on the other side" -- likely concerned with ritual purity (contact with a half-dead person would disqualify a priest for temple service under Leviticus 21:1-4). The priest's passage represents the inability of the Levitical system to address the fundamental human wound. Hebrews 7:11-12 makes the theological point: "if perfection could have been attained through the Levitical priesthood... why was there still need for another priest?" The Law's priest cannot complete the rescue that humanity needs. A better priest has come.
32: The Levite likewise "passed by on the other side" -- the double failure of the cultic system is the parable's first Christological move. The religious-cultic professionals who should be the agents of mercy cannot provide it. The Law can define the obligation ("love your neighbor") but it cannot produce the neighbor-love. Only a transformed heart (Jeremiah 31:33: "I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts") can produce genuine neighbor-love.
33: "But a Samaritan... when he saw him, he took pity on him" -- the Samaritan as hero is a provocation in the context of Jewish-Samaritan enmity (John 4:9). The Christological typological reading: Jesus himself is the despised one (John 8:48: "Are we not right in saying that you are a Samaritan and demon-possessed?") who crosses barriers of ethnic and religious hostility to rescue the wounded. "He took pity" (esplagchnisthē = was moved in his bowels, the deepest compassion) is the term used for Jesus's own compassion throughout the Gospels (Luke 7:13; Luke 15:20).
34: The Samaritan's specific acts: oil (healing) and wine (cleansing), the animal (giving up his own comfort), the inn (providing ongoing shelter), the personal care through the night. The Christological reading of the oil and wine: the oil of anointing (the Messiah = the anointed one) and the wine of the new covenant (Luke 22:20: "this cup is the new covenant in my blood"). The Samaritan's rescue is an enacted image of the atonement: the self-giving descent of the one who could have passed by, to lift up the one who cannot lift himself.
35: "When I return, I will reimburse you for any extra expense you may have" -- the promissory element of the rescue: the rescue is not complete at the initial intervention but requires ongoing provision and a promised return. The Christological typological reading (Augustine): the inn = the church; the innkeeper = the church's ministers; the two denarii = the provisions of Word and sacrament; the promised return = the Second Coming. The mapping carries genuine theological weight regardless of whether Jesus intended every detail allegorically.
36: "Which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers?" -- Jesus inverts the original question (Luke 10:29: "who is my neighbor?") into a question about enacted neighborliness. The lawyer asked "who counts as my neighbor?" -- a definitional question designed to limit obligation. Jesus asks "which one acted as a neighbor?" -- a volitional question that reframes obligation as action. Christ himself is the answer to the second question: the one who acted as neighbor to humanity when humanity could not act for itself.
37: "Go and do likewise" -- the command is not achievable by moral effort alone but by the Spirit that Christ sends. Paul writes that "love is the fulfillment of the law" (Romans 13:10) and that the Spirit who produces this love is "poured out into our hearts" (Romans 5:5). The "go and do likewise" is the Law's demand fulfilled in Christ and then enabled in those who receive his Spirit. The Good Samaritan parable ends not with a definition but with a commission: be what Christ is toward the world.
38: Martha opening her home to Jesus during the Jerusalem journey is an act of significant generosity and hospitality (philoxenia = love of strangers: Hebrews 13:2). The conflict that follows is not about hospitality vs. neglect but about priority of attention: between serving the Lord and sitting at his feet. Both are genuine goods; the question is their right order.
39: "Mary, who sat at the Lord's feet listening to what he said" -- sitting at the teacher's feet was the formal posture of a disciple (Acts 22:3: Paul educated "under Gamaliel"). A woman sitting at a rabbi's feet as a disciple was unconventional. "Listening to what he said" uses the singular "word" (logos): Mary is receiving the Word of God that Jesus embodies and speaks. The posture anticipates the "one necessary thing" Jesus will identify.
40: Martha "was distracted by all the preparations" (periespaōto peri pollēn diakonian = was being pulled around in many directions of service) -- diakonia (service/ministry) is significant: Martha's service is genuinely ministry. Luke uses diakonein/diakonia throughout Luke-Acts for significant service. Martha's complaint to Jesus reveals an anxiety about the service that transforms it from gift to burden. The distracting "many things" are contrasted with the "one thing" that Jesus will identify.
41: "Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things" -- the doubled name signals urgent personal concern, not rebuke. The anxiety (merimnas) is the same word used in the parable of the sower (Luke 8:14: "choked by life's worries") and in the prohibition of worry (Luke 12:22-31). The many things (pollon) contrast with the one thing (henos, Luke 10:42): Martha has allowed the multiplicity of service to obscure the singular priority that makes all service meaningful.
42: "Mary has chosen what is better, and it will not be taken away from her" -- the "one thing necessary" (henos de estin chreia) is sitting at Jesus's feet in the posture of a disciple receiving the Word. The promise "it will not be taken away from her" is the eschatological permanence of the better portion: the Kingdom received through hearing is permanent; everything else is temporary. This anticipates the one thing Paul counts as supreme gain (Philippians 3:8: "knowing Christ Jesus my Lord"). All service flows from and returns to this foundation.
